title: Reception Complaint Handling
description: How to defuse guest dissatisfaction at the front desk before it becomes a bad Booking review.

steps.title: Reading the complaint type
steps.content: Three types: material (broken shower, noise, dirt), service (rude staff, missed request), expectation (marketing promised more). The diagnosis drives the method. Material — fix fast. Service — apologize and compensate. Expectation — explain and offer alternatives.
steps.key_takeaway: Three complaint types = three different methods. Don't confuse them.

steps.title: HEAT: the defusing method
steps.content: H — Hear (listen silently, don't interrupt even if the guest is wrong). E — Empathize ('I understand, I'd feel the same'). A — Apologize (sincerely, once, no caveats). T — Take action (concrete action, not promises). HEAT is Marriott and IHG standard.
steps.key_takeaway: Hear → Empathize → Apologize → Take action. No 'but' between H and T.

steps.title: Responding to noise complaints
steps.content: The most common complaint. Solutions by escalation: offer earplugs → move to another room (away from elevator/kitchen/street) → free upgrade to a different room → 20-30% night discount. Never suggest 'bear with it' — that's provoking a bad review.
steps.key_takeaway: Four resolution levels. 'Bear with it' is not a solution — it's an invitation to a fight.

steps.title: Compensation tiers
steps.content: Tier 1 (agent): free breakfast, compliment (champagne, fruit), late check-out. Tier 2 (shift manager): room change, upgrade, up to 30% discount. Tier 3 (GM): refund, full compensation, Booking compensation. Don't exceed your tier.
steps.key_takeaway: Three compensation tiers. Yours = breakfast + compliment + late check-out.

steps.title: What NOT to say
steps.content: Banned phrases: 'Not my fault', 'This is the first time', 'Other guests haven't complained', 'Not against our standard', 'Read the rules'. Each one leads straight to a 1/10 Booking review. Replace with: 'I understand, let's fix this'.
steps.key_takeaway: Five killer phrases. Memorize them and never say them.

steps.title: Incident documentation
steps.content: After resolution: log in the PMS complaint register — date, room, issue, resolution, compensation, agent signature. For serious cases — photos, camera footage, manager report via Telegram/email. Documentation = protection during review and team training.
steps.key_takeaway: No record = no complaint. Which means the team won't learn.

steps.title: Follow-up and review prevention
steps.content: Two hours after resolution — verify all is well (call or drop-by). At check-out — personally apologize and ask about the experience. If the guest is happy with the resolution, ask: 'If today went well, a review would mean a lot'. This turns complaints into 5-star reviews 40% of the time.
steps.key_takeaway: A well-handled complaint creates a better review than a neutral stay.

quiz.question: What does HEAT stand for?
quiz.explanation: Standard Marriott and IHG method for defusing complaints.

quiz.question: What compensation is within an agent's authority?
quiz.explanation: Tier 1 — agent. Room change — shift manager. Refund — GM.

quiz.question: Which phrase is banned?
quiz.explanation: 'Other guests haven't complained' dismisses the guest and guarantees a bad review.

quiz.question: Where do you log incidents?
options: PMS complaint register + photos for serious cases
quiz.explanation: PMS + photos = hotel protection, team training, guest history.

quiz.question: What are the 4 resolution levels for noise complaints?
quiz.explanation: Four steps from cheap to expensive. 'Bear with it' is not on the list.

manager_checklist: Asks for review after successful resolution

kpi.how_to_measure: PMS log: resolution level / total complaints

kpi.target: ≤ 10% of complaints turn into a bad review
kpi.how_to_measure: Match guests from log with Booking reviews
